trajectory_summary: Parker runs a clean, efficient call on a cold customer and lands a tentative Monday booking in under six minutes. The structural beats are mostly hit, the credit threshold is stated correctly, and the tree-shading proactive surface is a genuine positive. However, the appointment is fragile: the son — confirmed co-owner and operational decision-maker — was never named, his contact info was never captured, and his availability was never confirmed. The call ends with Barbara's tentative preference, not a locked sit.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the observable outcome is a tentative booking with a structurally fragile foundation. The son is on the title, manages the electric account, and Barbara herself flagged she'd need to check with him — yet he is not confirmed on the appointment. This should be tagged as conditional_booking, not a clean sit, until the son's availability is verified.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Adult child co-owner inclusion — name, contact, confirmed availability
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, I don't know without checking with my son, but I can probably next Monday.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The son is on the title, manages the electric account, and Barbara herself flags she hasn't checked with him — yet Parker books the slot without capturing the son's name, contact info, or confirmed availability. The schema protocols.adult_child_decision_maker is explicit on this. This is the single highest-risk gap in the call: if the son isn't available Monday at 10AM, the sit doesn't happen and Parker doesn't get paid. The fix is a 15-second capture at 01:54 and a confirmation question at booking. Run this scenario with Parker in the 1-on-1: 'What happens Monday morning if the son has a conflict?'

key_moments.stewart_read: Barbara names her son as co-owner on the title. The schema protocols.adult_child_decision_maker is explicit: the correct response is to capture the son's name and ensure he is included in the appointment. Parker says 'Your son. Okay. Perfect.' and moves on — no name captured, no inclusion confirmed. Strategic hypothesis: Parker is deferring son-inclusion to the booking step. Reactive hypothesis: Parker missed the signal. The reactive read holds — the booking step (04:07–04:52) also fails to confirm the son's presence, so no later move was made.

key_moments.quote: Is that something you would be willing to trim back or cut down if it made more sense for your solar system?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker surfaces the tree proactively from satellite imagery and Barbara says she would 'consider' trimming or cutting it. This is a positive qualification signal — a customer willing to remove a shading obstacle is signaling real intent, not casual curiosity. Parker handles it adequately ('as long as you're willing to consider it, that's why we ask to at least get your design going') but doesn't anchor on it as a commitment signal or use it to deepen the interest conversation.

key_moments.quote: And then really the last thing, Barbara, I know you said your son does all the electric bill. The in order for us to build you a correct and accurate design, we do just look at your electric bill so you can see so we can see how much energy the home uses and how many panels you would need for the home.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker initiates bill collection correctly. When Barbara reveals she doesn't know the bill format and has only lived there a few months (03:26–03:47), Parker correctly identifies there is no usable bill history and pivots to booking at 03:55. The pivot decision is defensible — a customer with ~3 months of residency has no meaningful bill history to read back. The coaching failure is not the pivot itself but the absence of a handoff bridge: Parker never tells Barbara or the closer how the bill will be obtained. The closer will arrive Monday without bill data and no established expectation that they'll collect it on-site.

key_moments.quote: And then is the roof just a single roof there, an asphalt shingle roof?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker confirms roof type (asphalt shingle) but never asks roof age. The schema verify.roof_type_age requires both, with a hard DQ flag for roofs over 15 years. Barbara has only lived there a few months and may not know the roof age — but the question was never asked. A roof over 15 years old is a potential hard DQ or a roofing cross-sell opportunity; without the age, neither path can be evaluated.
